2026-05-28.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 client recovery mechanism where duplicate entries were being inserted into the reclaim hash table, leading to incorrect behavior during the grace period. The fix involves computing the string hash value first and then looking up the name in the hash table. If an existing entry is found, the new principal hash is folded into the existing record without allocating a new entry or modifying the hash table size. This ensures that the fast end-of-grace path works correctly and prevents dangling entries from being left in the hash table.

reviews.author: Chuck Lever
reviews.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever requested that the example in the commit message be revised to better match the actual path repaired by this patch, which involves a boot-time cld enumeration record followed by an in-grace RECLAIM_COMPLETE downcall for the same client name.
reviews.raw_body:
The motivating example in the commit message is built on the legacy
recovery-directory path, but the bug this patch fixes is nfsdcld-only,
which the Fixes: tag already reflects.

Could you recast the example around the path the patch actually
repairs -- a boot-time cld enumeration record followed by an in-grace
RECLAIM_COMPLETE downcall for the same cl_name -- where the records
are keyed consistently by cl_name and the dedup works?  The code
change itself looks correct.


-- 
Chuck Lever

2026-05-29.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 client reclaim functionality where a new entry is unconditionally allocated for each client, leading to incorrect behavior during the grace period. The fix involves computing the hash value first and then looking up the name in the hash table. If an existing entry is found, it is folded into the new record without allocating a new entry or modifying the size of the hash table. Additionally, the patch ensures that concurrent principal-hash checks are handled correctly by using smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire().
